coredata.dc:title: SOC estimation for lithium batteries using a CNN-attention-LSTM model
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Mei
Wang, Zhihui
coredata.dc:description: In order to improve the accuracy of state of charge (SOC) estimation for lithium batteries, a SOC prediction model based on CNN-Attention-LSTM is proposed. In this model, convolutional neural network (CNN) is used to extract the timing characteristics of battery voltage, current and temperature. Then, through the attention mechanism, dynamic weighting is performed to enhance the learning of key features. The dependencies in time-series data are then captured using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM). Finally, SOC parameters are output at the regression layer. In order to improve the efficiency and accuracy of model training, this paper introduces the Coronate Porcupine Optimization (CPO) to optimize the network hyperparameters. In this paper, SOC estimation experiments are performed under different temperatures, initial currents and datasets. The experimental results show that the MAE of the model is 0.84 % and 1.06 % at 50 °C and 0 °C, respectively. At 100 % and 60 % initial electricity, the MAE of the model is 1.09 % and 1.15 %, respectively, and the SOC estimation accuracy is significantly better than that of the LSTM and CNN-LSTM models. Furthermore, the model in this paper has high SOC estimation accuracy on different data sets, and the model shows good robustness and generalization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
SOC
Convolutional neural network
Long short-term memory network
Attention mechanism

SOC estimation experiments with varying initial charge levels

head.ce:title: SOC estimation for lithium batteries using a CNN-attention-LSTM model

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Electrical and Information Engineering, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan, Anhui, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Electrical and Information Engineering
Anhui University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Electrical and Information Engineering, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan, Anhui, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to improve the accuracy of state of charge (SOC) estimation for lithium batteries, a SOC prediction model based on CNN-Attention-LSTM is proposed. In this model, convolutional neural network (CNN) is used to extract the timing characteristics of battery voltage, current and temperature. Then, through the attention mechanism, dynamic weighting is performed to enhance the learning of key features. The dependencies in time-series data are then captured using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM). Finally, SOC parameters are output at the regression layer. In order to improve the efficiency and accuracy of model training, this paper introduces the Coronate Porcupine Optimization (CPO) to optimize the network hyperparameters. In this paper, SOC estimation experiments are performed under different temperatures, initial currents and datasets. The experimental results show that the MAE of the model is 0.84 % and 1.06 % at 50 °C and 0 °C, respectively. At 100 % and 60 % initial electricity, the MAE of the model is 1.09 % and 1.15 %, respectively, and the SOC estimation accuracy is significantly better than that of the LSTM and CNN-LSTM models. Furthermore, the model in this paper has high SOC estimation accuracy on different data sets, and the model shows good robustness and generalization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries have become the mainstream power source for electric vehicles due to their high power density, high energy density, long life and environmental protection characteristics [1–3]. An advanced battery management system (BMS) should be developed for monitoring the battery status in real time. This system allows electric vehicles to be more reliable and efficient [4]. In this system, SOC is a key parameter. It indicates the ratio of the current remaining power of the battery to the rated power, which can directly reflect the remaining capacity of the battery. Accurate SOC estimation can indicate the performance and accuracy of BMS, thus improving the reliability and efficiency of the entire BMS [5–7].

Currently, the primary methods for SOC estimation include traditional SOC prediction methods, model-based approaches, data-driven techniques, and hybrid methods. Among these, traditional SOC prediction methods mainly consist of the open-circuit voltage (OCV) method and the Ampere-Hour integral method. The OCV method [8,9] requires the battery to remain idle to measure the open-circuit voltage at a specific SOC level, making it unsuitable for online SOC estimation. The Ampere-Hour integral method [10,11], being an open-loop approach, suffers from cumulative errors, leading to limited accuracy in practical applications. Model-based SOC estimation methods primarily include electrochemical models and equivalent circuit models. Electrochemical models involve numerous partial differential equations, resulting in high computational complexity, which hinders their application in onboard BMS [12]. In contrast, electrochemical models feature a simpler structure with fewer parameters, making them the most widely adopted battery models. However, increasing the number of circuit components complicates parameter identification. Data-driven methods leverage historical data for model training, offering strong adaptability and flexibility. Hybrid approaches, which combine model-based methods with data-driven techniques, provide higher accuracy and adaptability but also have some drawbacks. Firstly, the integration of physical models with machine learning algorithms makes model design, training, and interpretation more complex. Secondly, hybrid methods depend on both accurate electrochemical or equivalent circuit models and large amounts of high-quality data, which are often difficult to obtain simultaneously in practical applications. Thirdly, the parameter tuning process for such models usually involves high computational costs and long processing times.

In data-driven methods, deep learning-based approaches have been widely applied to state of charge (SOC) estimation in recent years. Hannan et al. [13] proposed a CNN method with a learning rate optimization strategy. This method can achieve accurate SOC estimation under the condition of temperature change. Chen et al. [14] proposed an improved Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) network model. Under this model, the output volatility of RNN neural networks in SOC estimation can be effectively reduced by expanding the input and constraining the output. Liu et al. [15] proposed a method of SOC estimation based on CNN. This method can achieve high precision SOC prediction under different estimation conditions. In [16], Guo et al. conducted a comparative analysis of four advanced deep learning algorithms FCNN, LSTM, GRU, and TCN for SOC estimation, evaluating their performance across various aspects. Ma et al. proposed an LSTM-based joint SOC/SOE estimation method in [17], demonstrating through multi-scenario experiments its superior accuracy, robustness, and computational efficiency over traditional algorithms, making it suitable for complex battery management scenario. Ma et al. [18] introduced a deep learning-based input reconstruction-assisted approach, improving SOC estimation accuracy and robustness by incorporating unlabeled samples with a small amount of labeled data. In [19], Li et al. optimized the weights and thresholds of a CNN using particle swarm optimization (PSO), addressing the issue of traditional CNNs easily falling into local optima in SOC estimation. However, the study did not account for the effects of dynamic operating conditions and temperature variations in battery packs. In [20], Hong et al. employed a sparrow search algorithm to optimize LSTM hyperparameters, overcoming the insufficient accuracy of traditional SOC estimation methods under complex operating conditions. Nevertheless, this algorithm exhibits limitations when handling data from different temperature conditions.

To address the limitations and advantages of the existing methods mentioned above, this paper innovatively proposes a CNN-Attention-LSTM hybrid neural network model for lithium-ion battery SOC estimation. On the other hand, due to the network architecture incorporating convolutional layers, attention mechanisms, and long short-term memory units, the model involves numerous hyperparameters and is highly sensitive to initial parameters and the training process. If the hyperparameters are not properly selected, the model is prone to underfitting or overfitting, which adversely affects the accuracy and robustness of SOC estimation. This paper innovatively introduces the CPO algorithm to adaptively optimize the hyperparameters in the model, significantly improving training efficiency. The CPO algorithm, as an emerging intelligent optimization method, simulates the multiple defense strategies of the pangolin to achieve a dynamic balance between global exploration and local exploitation, effectively avoiding premature convergence to local optima. In the proposed model, the CNN extracts features from voltage, current, and temperature signals; the attention mechanism enables the network to focus more on important features; the LSTM layer captures temporal dependencies in the time-series data; the CPO algorithm automatically adjusts the hyperparameters of the network model; and the regression layer performs SOC prediction. Finally, SOC estimation experiments are conducted under various experimental conditions and different datasets to validate the effectiveness and robustness of the model.

The contributions of this study are summarized as follows:
(1)
A combined CNN-Attention-LSTM network model is proposed for capturing the nonlinear dynamics within the battery and estimating the battery's SOC through features related to voltage, current, and temperature.
(2)
The introduction of the CPO algorithm enables automatic hyperparameter tuning of the neural network model, thereby enhancing both training efficiency and SOC estimation accuracy.
(3)
The model is trained and compared with prevailing mainstream SOC estimation algorithms to assess its performance.
(4)
The robustness of the proposed model is examined under varying temperatures, initial charge levels, and different datasets, with SOC estimation results compared against those obtained from traditional LSTM and CNN-LSTM models.

The proposed CNN-Attention-LSTM model is designed for accurate battery SOC estimation, as depicted in Fig. 1 . The SOC prediction process primarily consists of four stages: data acquisition, model construction, model training, and model testing.
(1)
Data Acquisition: Experimental and publicly available data are collected. To verify the generalizability of the proposed algorithm, two datasets are used. Dataset A, provided by the Center for Advanced Life Cycle Engineering (CACLE), is utilized for both model training and testing. Dataset B, collected through an experimental setup in the laboratory, is used to validate the generalization performance of the trained model.
(2)
Model Construction: The CNN-Attention-LSTM model is constructed. The CNN extracts temporal features of inputs such as voltage, current, and temperature. The Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) attention mechanism module then enhances the weighting of key features. The LSTM processes the time-series data for SOC prediction.
(3)
Model Training: The training set from Dataset A is used to train the model, with the CPO algorithm optimizing the hyperparameters. The mean squared error (MSE) serves as the loss function, and network parameters are updated via the backpropagation algorithm.
(4)
Model Testing: The test set from Dataset A is used to evaluate the model. During the testing phase, both root mean squared error (RMSE) and mean absolute error (MAE) are employed to assess the model's performance, verifying its robustness and predictive accuracy under varying experimental conditions and operational states.
(5)
Generalization Performance Verification: Dataset B is used for a generalization test. The experimental data from Dataset B serve as inputs to assess the model's generalization capability through the CNN-Attention-LSTM model for SOC estimation.
A network model is designed, incorporating CNN-LSTM and an attention mechanism for the prediction of SOC in batteries, as illustrated in Fig. 2 .

Initially, the input battery voltage, current, and temperature data are fed into the input layer and the sequence folding layer in the form of time series. After being transformed into a two-dimensional structure suitable for convolution operations, the data are passed into the CNN module. The CNN module subsequently extracts high-dimensional spatial features through two convolutional layers, each followed by a ReLU activation layer. These convolution operations utilize local receptive fields and weight sharing mechanisms to effectively capture local correlation patterns within the data, thereby enhancing the model's representational capacity in the feature space.

After convolutional feature extraction, the model incorporates a SE attention mechanism to enhance its focus on critical feature channels, thereby compensating for CNN's limited capability in channel-wise recalibration. Specifically, the SE module takes the feature maps output by the CNN and performs global average pooling on each channel to obtain a global descriptor. This descriptor is then passed through a two-layer fully connected network to capture nonlinear channel dependencies. A sigmoid activation function is subsequently applied to generate normalized channel weights, which are multiplied with the original feature maps in a channel-wise manner to achieve dynamic feature recalibration. This mechanism explicitly models the importance of different feature channels for SOC estimation, effectively suppressing redundant information while emphasizing salient features.

The attention-modulated feature maps are then transformed back into time series form via a sequence unfolding layer and passed into the LSTM network, whose hyperparameters have been optimized using the CPO algorithm. CPO dynamically determines the optimal number of LSTM hidden units within a given range through an adaptive search strategy, balancing model complexity and temporal feature extraction capacity, and mitigating the limitations of manual hyperparameter tuning. The LSTM network, guided by the optimized number of hidden units, captures the temporal dependencies among voltage, current, and temperature data, and models the nonlinear dynamics of SOC evolution. Finally, the LSTM output is passed through a fully connected layer, where dimensionality is reduced and regularization is applied to prevent overfitting, thereby achieving high-precision SOC estimation.
The specific grid parameters of the CNN-Attention-LSTM model are detailed in Table 1 , where M denotes the total number of training samples.

The CNN is a type of feedforward neural network employed extensively in pattern recognition and feature extraction tasks [21,22]. A typical CNN architecture generally comprises an input layer, convolutional layers, pooling layers, fully connected layers, and an output layer [23]. The CNN module developed in this study is designed to extract multi-level spatial features from battery input data (voltage, current, and temperature), as depicted in Fig. 3 .

Initially, the module preprocesses the input data through a sequence folding operation (Seqfold), which compresses the time series dimensions to enhance feature extraction effectively. Subsequently, the data is processed through two convolutional layers with 1 × 1 kernels. The output channels of these layers are 32 and 64, respectively, progressively enhancing the capability to represent features. After each convolutional process, a Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation function is introduced to incorporate non-linearity, thereby improving the model's expressive power. Following this, a global average pooling layer pools the outputs of the convolutional layers to generate a global feature representation. This representation captures the global dependencies of the data. Finally, through two fully connected layers and a Sigmoid activation function, weights ranging from 0 to 1 are generated. These weights facilitate the channel attention mechanism, dynamically adjusting the importance of features.

The CNN module is effective in extracting both local patterns and global dependencies from the battery data. This provides high-quality feature inputs for subsequent temporal processing and SOC estimation, significantly enhancing the overall performance of the model.

An attention mechanism is a technique that allows a model to focus on the most important parts of the input sequence. This mechanism was initially widely used in natural language processing [24]. By introducing the attention mechanism, the model can dynamically adjust its attention to different input features, so as to extract and use these features for prediction more effectively [25].

In the model architecture proposed in this study, an SE attention module is designed and embedded between the CNN and LSTM layers. The SE attention mechanism, which was featured in the champion model of ImageNet 2017, facilitates the learning of dependencies among features, thereby augmenting the model's response to crucial features [26]. The schematic of the SE attention mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 4 .

The Squeeze operation performs global average pooling on the feature map Y to produce a vector 1 × 1 × C , as formulated in:
(1)
Z C
=
1
H × W
∑
i = 1
H
∑
j = 1
W
y c
i j

The Excitation operation employs the weight matrix W to generate the required weight information. Through two fully connected layers, W 1 and W 2, this operation processes the vector Z C obtained from the Squeeze operation to produce channel weight values s, as stipulated in:
(2)
s = σ
g
Z C
W
= σ
W 2
δ
W 1
Z C

The Scale operation updates the feature map Y with the weight vector s to produce the modified feature map
Y ′
, as defined in:
(3)
Y ′
= sY

A residual connection adds the input feature to the residual part, which has undergone down-sampling or channel adjustment. This addition facilitates information transmission and gradient flow, mitigating issues related to gradient vanishing or explosion.

The SE attention module dynamically reweights the feature maps across each channel, thereby enabling the model to focus more on features that are crucial for the estimation of SOC. Besides, irrelevant or redundant information is suppressed.

As a specialized variant of recurrent neural networks, LSTM networks are particularly adept at managing long-term dependencies in time-series data, making them highly suitable for SOC estimation tasks [27,28]. Consequently, this study adopts LSTM as the primary model for SOC estimation to enhance both prediction accuracy and robustness. Additionally, the CPO algorithm is utilized to refine its parameters, thereby augmenting the efficiency of model training.
(1)
LSTM Model

LSTM is a sophisticated deep learning architecture designed specifically for processing sequential data. It incorporates three distinct gates: the input gate, the forget gate, and the output gate, along with a cell state. These components collectively enable the LSTM to effectively manage long-term dependencies within data sequences by determining the retention of current information.

The LSTM unit employs these three gates to respectively determine the proportion of current cell memory integrating with the previous cell memory, the rate at which the current input is forgotten, and the impact of the cell memory on the node output [29]. The architecture of the LSTM model is depicted in Fig. 5 .

The computational formulas for the gates are as follows:
(4)
f t
= σ
W f
x t
+
U f
h
t − 1
+
b f
(5)
i t
= σ
W i
x t
+
U i
h
t − 1
+
b i
(6)
i t
= σ
W i
x t
+
U i
h
t − 1
+
b i
(7)
O t
= σ
W o

+
U o
h
t − 1
+
b o
(8)
C ~
t
= tanh
W c
x t
+
U c
h
t − 1
+
b c
(9)
C t
=
i t
⊗
C ~
t
+
f t
⊗
C
t − 1
(10)

=
O t

C t

In these equations,
W f
,
W i
,

,

,
U f
,
U i
,
U o
,
U c
represent the weight matrices that need to be learned during network training, and
b f
,
b i
,
b o
,
b c
are the bias vectors.
C t
and
C
t − 1
denote the current and the previous memory states, respectively, while
f t
,
i t
,
O t
are the activation vectors for the forget gate, input gate, and output gate. The activation functions used are σ (sigmoid) and tanh (hyperbolic tangent), with
h t
representing the hidden state outputted by the current LSTM unit.
(2)
CPO Algorithm

The CPO is a heuristic algorithm inspired by the defensive behaviors exhibited by crested porcupines. It is particularly designed for tackling complex nonlinear optimization problems. The CPO leverages these natural defense mechanisms, effectively balancing between global search and local exploitation, thus mitigating the risk of convergence to local optima. Central to its strategy are four defensive modalities: visual, auditory, olfactory, and physical [30].

Global Exploration Phase: During this phase, the CPO utilizes visual and auditory strategies to extensively probe the solution space, identifying areas with potential for optimization. It generates new solutions using the following formula, which facilitates a comprehensive search within the global context:
(11)
x i
t + 1
→
=
x i t
→
+
τ 1
×
2 ×
τ 2
×
x CP t
→
−
y i t
→

Here,
y t
represents the vector generated between the current CP and a random CP, and
x CP
denotes the optimal CP position.

Local Development Phase: In this phase, the CPO mimics the olfactory and physical defense strategies of crested porcupines to execute a focused search around the currently identified optimal solutions. Adjustments to the current solution are made according to the following formula, ensuring precise optimization within the local domain:
(12)
x i
t + 1
→
=
1 −
U 1
→
×
x i t
→
+
U 1
→
×
x
r 1
′
→
+
S i ′
×
x
r 2
′
→
−
x
r 3
′
→
−
τ 3
×
δ →
×
γ t
×
S i ′

In this context, δ is a parameter that governs the direction of search,
y t
acts as the defense factor, and
S t
represents the scent diffusion factor.

To sustain diversity within the population and avert premature convergence, the CPO algorithm incorporates a cyclic population reduction technique:
(13)
N =
N min
+
N ′
−
N min
×
1 −
t %
T max
T
T max
T

Here, T specifies the number of cycles, t indicates the current function evaluation,
T max
is the maximum number of function evaluations, and
N min
is the minimum number of individuals in the newly generated population.

This dynamic adjustment of the population size allows CPO to sustain diversity throughout the optimization process and avoid local optima.
(3)
Optimization of LSTM Using CPO for Lithium Battery SOC Estimation

In this study, the CPO algorithm is primarily employed to optimize three critical hyperparameters of the neural network model: the initial learning rate, the number of hidden units in the LSTM layer, and the L2 regularization coefficient.

The core control parameters of the algorithm are first configured, including the population size NN, the maximum number of iterations T
max , the dimensionality of the search space dim = 3, and the upper and lower bounds for each parameter. The initial positions of individuals are generated randomly within the defined boundaries using a uniform distribution, forming the initial population X∈R
N×dim . The fitness of each individual is then evaluated using a fitness function defined by the MSE of the neural network on the training dataset.

The iterative process of the CPO algorithm incorporates four defensive mechanisms that simulate the exploration and exploitation behaviors of crowned pigeons. During each iteration, different strategies are probabilistically selected to update the population positions. After each update, the algorithm checks and refreshes both the historical best positions of individuals and the global best position. To enhance convergence, the population size decreases progressively with each generation, simulating the natural contraction phenomenon of a population. The algorithm terminates upon reaching the maximum number of iterations or satisfying a convergence criterion, subsequently outputting the optimal set of parameters.

This optimal solution is then directly applied to the construction and training of the LSTM model, thereby accomplishing both hyperparameter tuning and model training. By introducing the CPO algorithm, the study achieves efficient optimization of key hyperparameters during network training, significantly improving both the model's fitting accuracy and generalization capability.

For the performance evaluation of 18650 lithium-ion batteries during charge-discharge cycles, the NEWARE battery testing system was employed. Monitored parameters included the relative duration, voltage, current, energy capacity, and power output during the charging, resting, and discharging phases. To ensure consistency in testing conditions, the batteries were placed in a thermostatic chamber set at 25 °C. Fig. 6 presents the experimental setup.
The specific battery parameters are detailed in Table 2 .

This study constructed two datasets for experimental purposes, designated as Dataset A and Dataset B. Dataset A was utilized for model training and testing, while Dataset B served to validate the generalizability of the trained model through SOC estimation.
(1)
Dataset A

Dataset A comprises publicly available data from CACLE, utilizing 18650 lithium iron phosphate batteries for charge-discharge experiments. Throughout the testing procedure, battery current, terminal voltage, and ambient temperature were recorded at 1-second intervals. Each discharge experiment commenced with a charge using a constant current of 1C and a constant voltage of 3.6 V until the charge termination current fell below 0.01C. Subsequently, discharge cycles employing Dynamic Stress Test (DST) and Federal Urban Driving Schedule (FUDS) were repeated to simulate various battery load behaviors until complete discharge occurred. Discharge was deemed complete when the terminal voltage dropped below the cutoff voltage of 2 V. Voltage and current waveforms for DST and FUDS discharge cycles are illustrated in Fig. 7 .
(2)
Dataset B

Dataset B contains data measured in the author's laboratory, with experimental equipment and procedures as previously described. Measured discharge voltage and current waveforms under HWFET and UDDS conditions are shown in Fig. 8 .

Prior to network training, the input data were preprocessed using the Mapminmax normalization method, which scales the data to the range of 0 to 1. The normalization formula is expressed as:
(14)
x =
x −
x min
x max
−
x min

In this formula,
x min
represents the minimum value of the input data, and
x max
is the maximum value.

Comprehensive experiments were conducted to assess the performance of the proposed model. During the experiment, the input variables of the model were the voltage, current, and temperature data collected under various battery operating conditions; the output variable was the SOC of the battery:
(1)
Model Comparison Experiment: To establish the superiority of the proposed model, it was compared with current mainstream algorithms for SOC estimation.
(2)
SOC Estimation Experiment at Various Temperatures: Given the significant impact of temperature variations on SOC estimation, the model's performance was analyzed under different temperature conditions.
(3)
SOC Estimation Experiment at Different Initial Charge Levels: Considering the uncertainty of initial SOC values in practical applications, this study further explored the model's SOC estimation capabilities under varying initial charge levels.
(4)
Generalization Experiment Across Different Datasets: To evaluate the model's generalization performance, SOC estimation was conducted using Dataset B.

During the training process, the MSE is employed as the overarching loss function evaluated at the conclusion of each forward pass. The formula for calculating MSE is as follows:
(15)
MSE =
∑
i = 1
n
y i
−
y ̂
i
2
n
where n represents the number of samples, y
i denotes the true value of the i-th sample, and
y ̂
i
is the predicted value of the i-th sample.

In the testing phase, model performance is assessed using metrics such as RMSE, MAE, and R2, with their respective formulas given by:
(16)
MAE =
∑
i = 1
n
y i
−
y ̂
i
n
(17)
RMSE =
∑
i = 1
n
y i
−
y ̂
i
2
n
(18)
R 2
= 1 −
∑
i = 1
n
y i
−
y ̂
i
2
∑
i = 1
n
y i
−
y ¯
2
where
y ¯
indicates the mean of the true values.

To validate the superiority of the proposed CNN-Attention-LSTM model, it is compared against the Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and LSTM models. The DST cycle at room temperature (25 °C) is used as the training set, and the FUDS cycle is used as the test set.
This experiment was conducted on MATLAB 2023a software, with the computational setup comprising an Intel CORE I7-13700H processor, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4060 Laptop GPU, and a memory capacity of 16 GB.
Fig. 9 presents the SOC estimation results of four models under room temperature conditions.

As indicated by Fig. 9, both ELM and SVM models exhibit significant fluctuations in the flat regions of the SOC curve, struggling to accurately capture the nonlinear characteristics; the LSTM model performs better than ELM and SVM, effectively capturing the trends in SOC changes, though it still exhibits some lag during rapid changes in SOC. In contrast, the proposed model delivers exceptionally high accuracy and stability in both the flat and dynamic regions of the SOC curve. This outcome demonstrates the model's effective handling of the nonlinear characteristics and dynamic load changes of lithium batteries, significantly surpassing traditional methods in estimation accuracy and robustness.
Table 3 further contrasts the estimation results of different models in terms of MAE, RMSE, and R2, providing a comprehensive and in-depth performance evaluation.

Data from Table 3 reveals that the proposed CNN-Attention-LSTM model excels in the evaluation of MAE and RMSE errors. Its MAE is only 1.09 %, and the RMSE is 1.47 %, significantly lower than the other three models. Moreover, the model's R2 value is as high as 0.9983, indicating the strongest fit to the actual SOC values.
Overall, the assessment of various errors demonstrates that the proposed model exhibits the smallest discrepancies, showcasing its exceptional performance in SOC estimation for batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental analysis of SOC estimation at various temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: The experiments for SOC estimation were conducted under two temperature conditions: low temperature at 0 °C and high temperature at 50 °C. For each temperature, the DST cycle served as the training dataset, while the FUDS cycle was used as the testing dataset. The test outcomes under different models are displayed in Fig. 10 .

In Fig. 10(a), it is observed that as the SOC declines to 0.5, the error margin begins to increase significantly. This phenomenon can be attributed to the substantial impact of low temperatures on the dynamic performance of batteries. Despite this, the proposed model demonstrates superior performance under cold conditions compared to traditional LSTM and CNN-LSTM models, indicating its robust adaptability to low temperatures.

In Fig. 10(b), the results of the proposed model appear more stable with less fluctuation in error. This stability is likely due to the accelerated chemical reaction rates and reduced internal resistance of the battery under high temperatures, which enhance its dynamic performance and thereby improve the model's estimation accuracy.
A comprehensive analysis of the results in Fig. 10(a) and (b) reveals that the proposed CNN-Attention-LSTM model exhibits high estimation accuracy under varying temperature conditions.
Table 4 further compares the MAE and RMSE of estimation results across different models.

As indicated in Table 4, under a high temperature of 50 °C, both MAE and RMSE for the proposed model show an improvement of 0.22 % and 0.34 %, respectively, compared to the performance at 0 °C. This improvement is reflective of the superior performance characteristics of batteries at higher temperatures. The proposed network effectively captures the influence of ambient temperature and provides reliable SOC estimations across different temperature settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SOC estimation experiments with varying initial charge levels
ce:section.ce:para: In practical applications, accurately determining the comfortable charge level of batteries is challenging. To validate the convergence capabilities of the model under different initial charge levels, SOC estimation experiments were conducted using battery data with initial charges set at 100 % and 60 %. The DST cycles with varying initial SOC levels at 25 °C were used as the training set, while the FUDS cycles served as the test set. The results are presented in Fig. 11 .

In Fig. 11(a), the model's estimation results almost perfectly align with the actual values when the initial SOC is 100 %, demonstrating exceptional accuracy and stability. This indicates that when the initial SOC is accurately known, the model rapidly attains a steady state and maintains a low estimation error throughout the charging and discharging process.
In Fig. 11(b), with an initial charge of 60 %, the model exhibits rapid convergence, adjusting quickly and closely approaching the true SOC values.
A combined analysis of the outcomes in Fig. 11(a) and (b) underscores that the proposed model consistently delivers high estimation accuracy and stability across different initial charge levels.
Table 5 provides a quantitative comparison of estimation errors (MAE/RMSE) across the evaluated models.

Table 5 reveals that the proposed model outperforms others in the evaluation of RMSE and MAE errors. With an initial charge of 100 %, the error values are the lowest at 1.32 % for RMSE and 1.09 % for MAE; with an initial charge of 60 %, the RMSE and MAE are recorded at 1.15 % and 0.89 %, respectively. These results demonstrate the model's effective handling of uncertainties in initial SOC values, meeting the complex demands of real-world applications.

To further verify the generalizability of the proposed CNN-Attention-LSTM model in practical application scenarios, this section conducts an SOC estimation experiment based on Dataset B. The UDDS condition is used as the training set, with the HWFET condition as the test set. The experimental results are depicted in Fig. 12 .

As shown in Fig. 12, the network proposed in this study also demonstrates superior performance on Dataset B. The RMSE is 1.13 %, and the MAE is 0.82 %, precisely tracking the dynamic trends of battery SOC. Similar to the experimental results on Dataset A, the prediction errors (MAE and RMSE) on Dataset B are maintained at a low level, further validating the algorithm's generalizability across different datasets.

SOC estimation faces challenges such as the difficulty in capturing long sequence data dependencies and insufficient extraction of feature space information. To address these issues, this study introduces a CNN-Attention-LSTM-based SOC estimation model and conducts related experiments, with the following conclusions:
(1)
Compared to existing models such as ELM, SVM, and LSTM, the proposed model achieves the highest SOC estimation accuracy, with RMSE and MAE reaching 1.47 % and 1.09 %, respectively. It effectively handles the non-linear characteristics and dynamic load changes of lithium batteries, offering higher estimation precision and robustness.
(2)
Under varying temperatures and different initial charge levels, the model exhibits excellent temperature adaptability and can converge rapidly even when the initial SOC value is unknown, significantly outperforming conventional methods. This validates the model's robustness under different conditions.
(3)
Across various datasets, the model maintains low levels of MAE and RMSE, adapting to multiple operational conditions and data distributions, thus demonstrating strong generalization capabilities.
Mei Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhihui Wang: Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Mei Zhang reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
